docs.title: Welcome to MAGDA
docs.text:
MAGDA (Multi Agent Digital Audio) is a free, open-source digital audio workstation (DAW) for recording, editing, and mixing audio and MIDI. Built with JUCE and Tracktion Engine, it provides a professional-grade audio engine in a lightweight, open-source package.

MAGDA includes a built-in AI assistant — describe what you want in natural language ("add a MIDI track with a bass clip", "transpose the selected notes up an octave") and MAGDA translates your request into actions. See AI Assistant to learn more.

docs.title: Three Views, One Workflow
docs.text: MAGDA is organized around three main views, each focused on a different stage of music production. Switch between them using the mode buttons in the footer bar.

docs.title: Session View (Live Mode)
docs.text:
A grid-based clip launcher for real-time performance and idea sketching. Trigger clips and scenes on a grid, with a mini mixer strip at the bottom of each track.

Go to Session View →

docs.title: Arrangement View (Arrange Mode)
docs.text:
A traditional timeline for composing, recording, and editing. Lay out clips on tracks along a horizontal timeline with full zoom, selection, and editing tools.

Go to Arrangement View →

docs.title: Mixer View (Mix Mode)
docs.text:
A channel-strip mixer for balancing levels, panning, routing, and adding effects. Each track gets a full-height fader with metering, I/O routing, and send slots.

Go to Mixer View →

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Tracks — Hybrid tracks that accept any clip type, with FX chains and racks for flexible routing
  • Built-in Devices — Drum Grid, Sampler, synths, and effects included out of the box
  • Modulation — LFOs, bezier curve shapes, and macros for dynamic parameter control
  • Interface Layout — Collapsible panels, context-sensitive editors, and plugin/media browsers

Visit magda.land for downloads and more information.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
New to MAGDA? Start here:

  1. Installation — Download, build, and set up MAGDA
  2. First Session — Create a project, add tracks, and make some noise

docs.title: Arrangement View
docs.text: The Arrangement View is a traditional DAW timeline for composing, recording, and editing. Switch to it by clicking Arrange in the footer bar.

docs.title: Layout
docs.text:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Timeline ruler (bars / beats / time)        │
├──────────┬──────────────────────────────────┤
│ Track    │                                  │
│ Headers  │   Track Content (clips on        │
│          │   the timeline)                  │
│  Name    │                                  │
│  Color   │         ▼ Playhead               │
│  M S R   │                                  │
├──────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
│ Aux      │   Aux track content              │
├──────────┼──────────────────────────────────┤
│ Master   │   Master waveform                │
└──────────┴──────────────────────────────────┘
  • Track headers (left) — Track name, color, mute/solo controls. Resizable width.
  • Track content (center) — Clips displayed on the timeline. Scrollable horizontally and vertically.
  • Timeline ruler (top) — Shows bars, beats, and time markers.
  • Playhead — Vertical line showing the current playback position, always on top.
  • Aux tracks — Auxiliary/bus tracks in a fixed section above the master.
  • Master track (bottom) — Master output with its own resizable row.

docs.title: Toolbar
docs.text:
The corner toolbar provides quick access to:

  • Zoom to fit / Zoom to selection / Zoom to loop
  • Track size presets (small, medium, large)
  • Add track
  • I/O visibility toggle

docs.title: Working with Clips
docs.text:
• Move — Click and drag a clip along the timeline or between tracks
  • Resize — Drag the edges of a clip to trim it
  • Select — Click a clip to select it; Ctrl+A (Cmd+A) to select all
  • Delete — Select a clip and press Del or Backspace
  • Duplicate — Ctrl+D (Cmd+D) to duplicate selected clips

Drag audio files from the Media Explorer directly onto a track to import them as clips.

docs.title: Scrolling
docs.text:
• Horizontal scroll — Move along the timeline (mouse wheel or scroll gesture)
  • Vertical scroll — Navigate between tracks

docs.title: Zooming
docs.text:
• Horizontal zoom — Ctrl++ / Ctrl+- (Cmd++ / Cmd+- on macOS) or use the zoom scrollbar
  • Vertical zoom — Change track height using the toolbar presets (small, medium, large)

docs.title: Toolbar Zoom Actions
docs.text:
• Zoom to fit — Fit all content in the visible area
  • Zoom to selection — Zoom into the current time selection
  • Zoom to loop — Zoom to the loop region

docs.title: Selection
docs.text:
• Time selection — Click and drag on the timeline ruler to select a time range
  • Loop region — Displayed as an overlay on the timeline; set from selection with Ctrl+Shift+L (Cmd+Shift+L)

docs.title: Playhead
docs.text:
The playhead shows the current playback position. Click on the timeline ruler to reposition it. Press Home to return to the start.

Edit Cursor

The edit cursor is a white blinking line separate from the playhead. Place it with Alt+click on the timeline ruler or inside a clip. It marks the position for operations like Split (Cmd+E). Press Esc to hide it. The MIDI editor has its own independent edit cursor — placing one in the piano roll or drum grid does not affect the arrangement cursor.

docs.title: Editing Modes
docs.text: MAGDA supports two editing modes depending on context:

docs.title: Clip-Based Editing
docs.text:
When no time selection is active, editing operations apply to selected clips:

  • Split (Cmd+E) — Splits selected clips at the edit cursor position
  • Render (Cmd+B) — Renders selected clips to audio in place
  • Cut / Copy / Paste / Duplicate / Delete — Operate on the selected clips

docs.title: Time Selection Editing
docs.text:
When a time selection is active (drag on the timeline ruler), operations apply to the time range:

  • Split / Trim (Cmd+E) — Splits clips at the selection boundaries and isolates the selected region. If clips extend beyond the selection, they are trimmed.
  • Render Time Selection (Cmd+Shift+B) — Consolidates everything within the time selection to a single audio clip per track
  • Cut / Copy / Paste — Operate on the content within the time range

Note

When a time selection exists, Split/Trim affects all clips that overlap the selection — not just selected clips. Clear the time selection to return to clip-based editing.

docs.title: Cut, Copy, Paste
docs.text: Action Shortcut Cut Ctrl+X / Cmd+X Copy Ctrl+C / Cmd+C Paste Ctrl+V / Cmd+V

docs.title: Split and Join
docs.text:
• Split — Position the edit cursor and press Ctrl+E (Cmd+E) to split the clip at that point. If a time selection is active, clips are trimmed to the selection.
  • Join — Select adjacent clips and press Ctrl+J (Cmd+J) to merge them.

docs.title: Duplicate
docs.text:
• Duplicate clips — Ctrl+D (Cmd+D) duplicates selected clips
  • Duplicate track — Ctrl+D with a track selected duplicates the track and its content
  • Duplicate track (empty) — Ctrl+Shift+D (Cmd+Shift+D) duplicates the track structure without content

docs.title: Render
docs.text:
• Render clip to audio — Select a clip and press Ctrl+B (Cmd+B) to bounce it to an audio file
  • Render time selection — Press Ctrl+Shift+B (Cmd+Shift+B) to render the selected time range

docs.title: Undo / Redo
docs.text: Action Shortcut Undo Ctrl+Z / Cmd+Z Redo Ctrl+Shift+Z / Cmd+Shift+Z

docs.title: Clips
docs.text: Clips are the containers that hold audio and MIDI data on tracks. They appear as rectangular blocks in the Arrangement View timeline and as trigger pads in Session View.

docs.title: Clip Types
docs.text:
Type Description Audio Contains a reference to an audio file (WAV, AIFF, FLAC, MP3, OGG) with start/end points and gain MIDI Contains MIDI note and automation data

Because MAGDA uses a hybrid track system, any track can hold both audio and MIDI clips.

docs.title: MIDI Clip Properties
docs.text: Property Description Name Display name of the clip Colour Clip colour in the editor Start Start position in beats Length Duration in beats Offset Non-destructive trim offset in beats Transpose Pitch shift from -24 to +24 semitones

docs.title: Audio Clip Properties
docs.text: Property Description Name Display name of the clip Colour Clip colour in the editor Start Start position on the timeline Length Duration Offset Start position in the source file for non-destructive trimming Speed Ratio Playback speed factor (0.25x to 4.0x) Loop Enable looping with configurable loop start and length Warp Enable warp markers for per-segment time-stretching Auto Tempo Lock clip to project tempo (beat mode) Source BPM Original tempo of the source file Transpose Manual pitch shift from -24 to +24 semitones Auto-Pitch Automatic pitch tracking (Pitch Track, Chord Mono, Chord Poly) Analog Pitch Resample instead of time-stretch for pitch changes Volume Clip volume in dB Gain Additional boost from 0 to +24 dB Pan Stereo position from L to R Reverse Play audio backwards Fade In / Out Fade duration and curve type (Linear, Convex, Concave, S-Curve) Auto Crossfade Automatic crossfade with adjacent clips

docs.title: Session Clip Properties
docs.text:
Both MIDI and audio clips in Session View have additional launch properties:

Property Description Launch Mode Trigger (one-shot) or Toggle (on/off) Launch Quantize Quantize launch timing (None, 8 Bars, 4 Bars, 2 Bars, 1 Bar, 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, 1/16)

Note

Session clips do not have an absolute position property — they are not placed on the timeline. Their position is determined by the scene slot they occupy.

docs.title: Creating Clips
docs.text:
• Double-click an empty area on a track to create a new 1-bar MIDI clip at that position
  • Double-click over a time selection to create a MIDI clip matching the selection length

docs.title: Selecting Clips
docs.text:
• Click a clip to select it
  • Shift+click to add or remove clips from the selection
  • Cmd+A (Ctrl+A on Windows/Linux) selects all clips in the arrangement, or all notes when a MIDI editor is focused

docs.title: Editing Clips
docs.text:
• Move — Drag a clip to reposition it on the timeline or move it to another track
  • Resize — Drag the left or right edge of a clip to trim its start or end point
  • Duplicate — Hold Alt and drag a clip, or press Cmd+D
  • Split — Position the playhead and use Edit > Split Clip or press Cmd+E
  • Delete — Select the clip and press Del or Backspace

docs.title: Cut, Copy, and Paste
docs.text:
• Cmd+X — Cut the selected clip(s)
  • Cmd+C — Copy the selected clip(s)
  • Cmd+V — Paste at the playhead position on the same track

docs.title: Audio Clip Modes
docs.text: Audio clips have several playback modes that control how the clip responds to tempo changes and pitch adjustments. Set the mode in the clip's Inspector panel.

docs.title: Raw (Default)
docs.text: The clip plays back at its original speed and pitch, unaffected by the project tempo. Changing the project tempo does not stretch or compress the clip. This is the simplest mode — what you recorded or imported is what you hear.

docs.title: Beat Mode
docs.text: Locks the clip's length to a musical duration (e.g. 4 bars). When the project tempo changes, MAGDA time-stretches the audio so it always fills the same number of bars. Use this for loops, drum patterns, and rhythmic material that should stay in sync with the project.

docs.title: Repitch Mode
docs.text: Adjusts playback speed to match tempo changes — like speeding up or slowing down a vinyl record. Faster tempo raises pitch, slower tempo lowers it. No time-stretching artefacts, but the pitch shifts with tempo. Useful for lo-fi effects or when pitch drift is acceptable.

docs.title: Warp Mode
docs.text:
Enables per-segment time-stretching using warp markers. Each marker pins a point in the audio to a specific position on the timeline. The audio between markers is independently stretched or compressed. This is the most flexible mode — use it to align a free-tempo recording to the grid, fix timing, or create creative effects.

  • Add a warp marker — Double-click the waveform at the desired position
  • Move a warp marker — Drag it left or right to shift that section of audio earlier or later
  • Delete a warp marker — Double-click an existing marker, or right-click and select Remove

docs.title: Slicing
docs.text:
Right-click an audio clip to access slice operations. These split the clip into multiple pieces based on warp markers or the current grid setting.

  • Slice at Warp Markers In Place — Split the clip at each warp marker position, creating separate clips on the same track. Requires warp mode with at least one user-placed marker.
  • Slice at Grid In Place — Split the clip at every grid line, creating separate clips on the same track.
  • Slice at Warp Markers to Drum Grid — Create a new Drum Grid track where each slice between warp markers becomes a pad, with a MIDI pattern that reproduces the original timing.
  • Slice at Grid to Drum Grid — Same as above but slicing at grid intervals instead of warp markers.

docs.title: Time-Stretch Algorithm
docs.text: When Beat or Warp mode is active, MAGDA uses the SoundTouch algorithm for time-stretching.

docs.title: Freeze, Bounce, and Render
docs.text:
These operations render a track or clip's output to audio, useful for reducing CPU load or committing effects.

  • Freeze — Render the track's output to a temporary audio file to free up CPU. The track becomes read-only until unfrozen. Use Track > Freeze Track.
  • Bounce in Place — Render the track to a new audio clip that replaces the original content. Use Track > Bounce in Place.
  • Bounce to New Track — Render the track's output to a new audio track, preserving the original track unchanged. Use Track > Bounce to New Track.

docs.title: FX Chain & Racks
docs.text: Each track has an FX chain — an ordered list of audio processors applied to the track's signal.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The FX chain is displayed in the bottom panel when a track or device is selected. Signal flows left to right through the chain.

Note

The chain header provides access to the track tree , a hierarchical view of all devices and racks on the track. You can also click the rack button to create a new rack in the chain.

The chain can contain:

  • Plugins — VST3, AU, or VST effect and instrument plugins
  • Built-in devices — MAGDA's own processors (see Built-in Devices)
  • Racks — Container devices with nested chains and parallel routing

docs.title: Working with Devices
docs.text:
• Drag plugins from the Plugin Browser onto the chain to add them
  • Drag devices to reorder them in the chain
  • Click a device to select it and show its parameters
  • Right-click a device for options: bypass, remove, replace, move to rack
  • Each device has Mod and Macro buttons to toggle the modulation and macro panels

docs.title: Racks
docs.text: A rack is a container that holds one or more parallel chains. Signal flows into the rack, splits across chains, and mixes back together at the output.

docs.title: Creating a Rack
docs.text:
• Click the rack button in the chain header
  • Right-click a device and select Move to Rack to wrap it in a new rack

docs.title: Chains
docs.text:
Each rack contains one or more chains displayed as rows. Each chain has:

  • Volume — Level control for the chain's output
  • Pan — Stereo positioning of the chain
  • Mute (M) / Solo (S) — Mute or solo individual chains
  • Devices — Each chain has its own ordered list of devices

Click the + button next to "Chains" to add a new parallel chain.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Parallel processing — Blend dry and wet signals (e.g., parallel compression)
  • Multi-band processing — Split signal into frequency bands with different processing
  • Organized grouping — Keep related devices together in a collapsible container

docs.title: Rack Controls
docs.text:
• Volume — Master output level for the entire rack
  • Mod / Macro buttons — Access the rack's own modulation and macro panels
  • Collapse/Expand — Click the rack header to toggle between collapsed and expanded views

docs.title: Collapsible Devices
docs.text: Individual devices inside a rack can be collapsed to save space. Click the collapse button on a device to shrink it to a compact header. The rack resizes dynamically as devices are collapsed or expanded. Collapsed states are preserved when the chain rebuilds (e.g. when adding or removing devices).

docs.title: Mixer View
docs.text: The Mixer View provides a channel-strip interface for balancing levels, panning, routing, and applying effects. Switch to it by clicking Mix in the footer bar.

docs.title: Layout
docs.text:
┌──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┬─────────┐
│ Track 1  │ Track 2  │ Track 3  │ Aux 1    │ Master  │
│          │          │          │          │         │
│ [Routing]│ [Routing]│ [Routing]│ [Routing]│         │
│ [Sends]  │ [Sends]  │ [Sends]  │ [Sends]  │         │
│          │          │          │          │         │
│  ┃  ┃    │  ┃  ┃    │  ┃  ┃    │  ┃  ┃    │  ┃  ┃   │
│  ┃██┃    │  ┃█ ┃    │  ┃  ┃    │  ┃█ ┃    │  ┃██┃   │
│  ┃██┃    │  ┃██┃    │  ┃█ ┃    │  ┃██┃    │  ┃██┃   │
│  ┃██┃    │  ┃██┃    │  ┃██┃    │  ┃██┃    │  ┃██┃   │
│  [Pan]   │  [Pan]   │  [Pan]   │  [Pan]   │  [Pan]  │
│  M S R   │  M S R   │  M S R   │  M S     │         │
│ Track 1  │ Track 2  │ Track 3  │ Aux 1    │ Master  │
└──────────┴──────────┴──────────┴──────────┴─────────┘
  • Channel strips — One per track, scrollable horizontally
  • Aux channel strips — Auxiliary/bus tracks
  • Master channel strip — Final output, fixed on the right
  • Resizable width — Drag the edge of a channel strip to resize

Click a channel strip to select it. Drag plugins onto a channel strip to add them to the track's FX chain.

docs.title: Channel Strip Controls
docs.text: Each channel strip provides:

docs.title: Volume Fader
docs.text: Drag the fader to adjust the track's output level. The dB scale is displayed alongside the fader.

docs.title: Level Meters
docs.text: Stereo level meters (L/R) show the real-time signal level. A peak label displays the highest level reached — watch for clipping.

docs.title: Pan
docs.text: The pan control positions the track in the stereo field, from hard left to hard right.

docs.title: Mute, Solo, Record
docs.text:
• Mute (M) — Silence the track output
  • Solo (S) — Solo this track, muting all others
  • Record (R) — Arm the track for recording
  • Monitor — Enable input monitoring

docs.title: Track Name and Color
docs.text: The track name and color bar are displayed at the bottom of each strip for identification.

docs.title: Master Channel Strip
docs.text: The master strip controls the final output level of the mix. It has its own volume fader, meters, and pan control but no mute/solo/record buttons.

docs.title: I/O Routing
docs.text:
Each channel strip has routing selectors for:

  • Audio input — Select which physical input or bus feeds the track
  • Audio output — Choose where the track's audio is sent (master, aux bus, etc.)
  • MIDI input — Select the MIDI input device and channel
  • MIDI output — Route MIDI to a specific device or virtual instrument

docs.title: Sends
docs.text:
Each channel strip has a send section for routing audio to auxiliary buses.

  • Add a send — Create a new send slot to route signal to an aux track
  • Send level — Adjust how much signal is sent to the aux bus
  • Remove a send — Click the remove button on a send slot to delete it

The send section is resizable — drag the resize handle to show more or fewer send slots.

docs.title: Multi-Output Plugins
docs.text: When an instrument has multiple output pairs activated, each output appears as its own channel strip in the mixer — just like any other track. You can route these strips to groups or aux sends independently. See Multi-Output Plugins for setup details.

docs.title: DrumGrid Sub-Channels
docs.text: When a track contains a DrumGrid device with multiple outputs, the mixer can expand to show individual sub-channel strips for each drum voice, giving you independent level and pan control per pad output. See Drum Grid for details.

docs.title: Session View
docs.text: The Session View is a grid-based clip launcher for real-time performance and idea sketching. Switch to it by clicking Live in the footer bar.

docs.title: Layout
docs.text:
The Session View is organized as a grid:

  • Columns represent tracks
  • Rows represent scenes
  • Each cell is a clip slot that can hold an audio or MIDI clip
 Track 1    Track 2    Track 3   │ Scene
┌──────────┬──────────┬──────────┼────────┐
│ Clip A   │          │ Clip D   │ ► 1    │
├──────────┼──────────┼──────────┼────────┤
│          │ Clip B   │ Clip E   │ ► 2    │
├──────────┼──────────┼──────────┼────────┤
│ Clip C   │          │          │ ► 3    │
├──────────┼──────────┼──────────┼────────┤
│ Fader    │ Fader    │ Fader    │ ■ Stop │
└──────────┴──────────┴──────────┴────────┘
  • Track headers run across the top — click to select a track
  • Scene launch buttons on the right trigger all clips in a row simultaneously
  • Stop All button (■) stops all playing clips
  • Mini mixer fader row at the bottom provides quick mixing controls

docs.title: Clip Slots
docs.text:
Each cell in the grid is a clip slot. A slot can be empty or contain a clip.

  • Click an occupied slot to trigger (play) the clip
  • Click a playing slot to stop it
  • Only one clip per track plays at a time — triggering a new clip in the same column stops the previous one

docs.title: Scenes
docs.text:
A scene is a horizontal row across all tracks. Launching a scene triggers all clips in that row simultaneously, which is useful for transitioning between song sections during a live performance.

  • Click the scene launch button (►) on the right side to launch a scene
  • Use the Stop All button (■) to stop all playing clips at once

docs.title: Mini Mixer
docs.text:
At the bottom of the Session View, each track has a mini channel strip for quick mixing without leaving the clip launcher.

Each mini channel strip provides:

  • Volume fader — Adjust track level
  • Pan knob — Position in the stereo field
  • Mute (M) — Silence the track
  • Solo (S) — Listen to only this track

A master strip is also available for the main output level.

The fader row is resizable — drag the top edge to make it taller or shorter.

docs.title: I/O and Send Rows
docs.text: Optional rows for I/O routing and send levels can be toggled on or off to keep the view compact or show more detail.

docs.title: Drag and Drop
docs.text:
• Drag audio files from the Media Explorer onto a clip slot to import them
  • Drag plugins onto a track header to add effects

docs.title: Adding Clips
docs.text:
• Drag and drop audio or MIDI files from the Media Explorer onto an empty clip slot
  • Record into the arrangement and move clips to the session grid

Note

Clip launching quantize and follow-action settings are configured in the Clip Inspector.

docs.title: Tracks
docs.text: Tracks are the fundamental building blocks in MAGDA. They appear in all three views — as columns in Session View, rows in Arrangement View, and channel strips in Mixer View.

docs.title: Hybrid Track System
docs.text: MAGDA uses a hybrid track system: there is no strict distinction between audio and MIDI tracks. Any track can contain any combination of audio clips, MIDI clips, and other clip types. The track's behavior adapts based on the clips and devices it contains.

docs.title: Track Types
docs.text: Type Description Audio Standard track for audio recording and playback Instrument Track with a virtual instrument plugin loaded MIDI Track that sends MIDI to external devices Group Bus track that groups multiple child tracks Aux Auxiliary/send-return track for shared effects Master Final stereo output — one per project

docs.title: Track Controls
docs.text:
Every track provides the following controls (visible in track headers and channel strips):

  • Volume fader — Adjust the track's output level
  • Pan knob — Position in the stereo field
  • Mute (M) — Silence the track. Shortcut: select the track and press M
  • Solo (S) — Solo the track. Shortcut: select the track and press Shift+S
  • Record arm (R) — Arm the track for recording
  • Input monitor — Monitor the live input signal through the track
  • Automation — Toggle automation read/write for the track

docs.title: Multi-Output Plugins
docs.text:
When an instrument plugin has more than two output channels (e.g. Kontakt, Battery, Drum Machine Designer), MAGDA can create separate output tracks for each stereo pair.

  • Open the multi-out menu on the device (the button) and activate the output pairs you need
  • Each activated pair creates an independent track placed directly after the instrument track
  • Output tracks behave like any other track — you can rename, reorder, route to groups, and apply FX
  • To organize them, select the output tracks and move them into a Group using the right-click menu

Note

Multi-out tracks receive audio from the parent instrument's rack — their input routing is fixed. Output routing (to master, groups, or aux sends) works normally.

docs.title: Track View Manager
docs.text:
Each of the three views — Session, Arrangement, and Mixer — maintains its own independent track visibility and layout. This means you can show a different set of tracks in each view.

Open the Track Manager from the View menu to configure track visibility per view. Each column (Live, Arrange, Mix) has independent checkboxes — toggle them to control which tracks appear in each view.

Per-view track settings include:

  • Visible — Whether the track appears in this view
  • Locked — Prevent editing the track in this view
  • Collapsed — For group tracks: collapse or expand children
  • Height — Track row height (Arrangement View)

docs.title: Example: Multi-Output Workflow
docs.text:
A common use case is keeping the Arrangement View clean while having full control in the Mixer. With a multi-output instrument like Addictive Drums, you can hide the individual output tracks from the Arrangement and show only the MIDI track you compose on:

Arrangement View — Only the group and the instrument's MIDI track are visible, keeping the timeline uncluttered:

Mixer View — All output tracks are visible for independent level, pan, and effects control per drum voice:

docs.title: Adding and Managing Tracks
docs.text:
• Press Ctrl+T (Cmd+T on macOS) to add a new track
  • Right-click a track header for options: rename, duplicate, delete, freeze
  • Click the track name to rename it

docs.title: FX Chain
docs.text: Each track has an FX chain — an ordered list of audio processors applied to the track's signal. See FX Chain & Racks for full details.

docs.title: Clips
docs.text: Tracks contain clips — audio and MIDI data blocks arranged on the timeline. See Clips for details on editing, splitting, duplicating, and rendering clips.

docs.title: Transport
docs.text: The transport bar sits at the top of the window and is available in all views.

docs.title: Navigation & Playback
docs.text:
From left to right, the transport buttons are:

Button Action Shortcut Home Return to the beginning Home Previous Jump to previous marker Next Jump to next marker Play Start/stop playback Space Stop Stop and reset Space Record Start/stop recording R Loop Toggle loop playback Back to Arrangement Return from session clip playback to the arrangement position

docs.title: Loop
docs.text:
Enable looping with the Loop button in the transport. The loop region is defined by start and end points, displayed as editable time labels (bars:beats:ticks).

  • Set loop from a time selection — Make a time selection in the arrangement and press L to set the loop region to match the selection
  • Set loop from a clip — Select the clip and press Ctrl+Shift+L (Cmd+Shift+L)

docs.title: Punch In/Out
docs.text: Use punch in and punch out to record only within a specific time range. Enable punch mode and set the punch region start and end points in the transport bar.

docs.title: Position Displays
docs.text:
The transport shows editable displays for:

  • Playhead position — Current playback time
  • Edit cursor position — Position of the edit cursor
  • Time selection — Start and end of the current selection

docs.title: Tempo
docs.text:
The tempo (BPM) is displayed in the transport bar. You can change it by:

  • Clicking and dragging the tempo value up or down
  • Clicking the tempo label and typing a new value

docs.title: Time Signature
docs.text: The time signature is displayed alongside the tempo in the transport bar.

docs.title: Grid & Snap
docs.text:
The grid controls how clips and edits snap to musical divisions.

  • Snap toggle — Enable or disable snapping to the grid
  • Grid quantize — Set the grid resolution (e.g., 1/4, 1/8, 1/16 notes) using the numerator and denominator controls
  • Auto-grid — Automatically adjust the grid resolution based on the current zoom level

docs.title: Built-in Devices
docs.text: MAGDA includes its own custom devices as well as processors from the Tracktion Engine.

docs.title: MAGDA Devices
docs.text: Device Description Drum Grid Chain-based drum machine with per-pad FX. See Drum Grid.

docs.title: Tracktion Engine Devices
docs.text:
These processors are provided by the Tracktion Engine and are available in every MAGDA installation:

Device Description Sampler 8-voice sample player with ADSR and pitch controls. See Sampler. 4OSC Synth Four-oscillator subtractive synthesizer EQ Parametric equalizer Compressor Dynamics compressor Reverb Algorithmic reverb Delay Tempo-synced delay Chorus Chorus/ensemble effect Phaser Phaser effect Filter Multimode filter (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass) Pitch Shift Real-time pitch shifting IR Reverb Convolution reverb using impulse responses Utility Gain, phase invert, stereo width Test Tone Signal generator for testing and calibration

docs.title: External Plugins
docs.text:
In addition to built-in devices, MAGDA supports external plugins in the following formats:

  • VST3 — Cross-platform
  • Audio Units (AU) — macOS only
  • VST — Legacy format, cross-platform

Use the Plugin Browser to find and add plugins to your tracks.

docs.title: Drum Grid
docs.text: The Drum Grid is MAGDA's chain-based drum machine. Each pad is a full audio chain, giving you independent processing per drum voice.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Drum Grid maps incoming MIDI notes to individual pads. Each pad has its own sample, FX chain, and mixer controls, making it a self-contained drum kit in a single device.

docs.title: Pads
docs.text:
• Each pad is mapped to a specific MIDI note
  • Pads can be triggered from the Piano Roll, the Drum Grid Editor (bottom panel), or a MIDI controller
  • Click a pad to select it and view its chain in the Inspector

docs.title: Per-Pad FX Chain
docs.text: Each pad has its own FX chain, just like a regular track. You can add plugins, built-in devices, or racks to any pad's chain. This allows effects like distortion on a snare without affecting the kick.

docs.title: Per-Pad Mix
docs.text:
Each pad has independent mix controls:

  • Volume — Pad output level
  • Pan — Stereo position
  • Output routing — Route individual pads to separate mixer channels for independent mixing

When a DrumGrid track is expanded in the Mixer View, each pad appears as its own sub-channel strip.

docs.title: Adding Samples
docs.text:
• Drag audio files from the Media Explorer onto a pad to load a sample
  • Each pad plays one sample at a time

docs.title: MIDI Mapping
docs.text: The default mapping assigns pads to General MIDI drum note numbers (kick = C1, snare = D1, etc.), but pads can be remapped to any MIDI note.

docs.title: Sampler
docs.text: The Sampler is MAGDA's built-in sample playback instrument with multi-voice support and synthesis controls.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Load an audio sample and play it chromatically across the keyboard. The Sampler supports up to 8 simultaneous voices (polyphony).

docs.title: Loading Samples
docs.text:
• Drag an audio file from the Media Explorer onto the Sampler device
  • Or click the file browser button within the Sampler UI to select a file

docs.title: Pitch
docs.text:
• Root note — The MIDI note at which the sample plays at its original pitch
  • Pitch — Transpose in semitones
  • Fine-tune — Detune in cents

docs.title: Sample Region
docs.text:
• Start — Sample playback start point
  • End — Sample playback end point
  • Drag the start/end markers in the waveform display to define the playback region

docs.title: ADSR Envelope
docs.text:
• Attack — Time for the sound to reach full level after a note-on
  • Decay — Time to fall from peak to sustain level
  • Sustain — Level held while the note is held
  • Release — Time to fade out after note-off

docs.title: Voices
docs.text:
• Polyphony — Up to 8 simultaneous voices
  • When the voice limit is reached, the oldest note is stolen

docs.title: First Session
docs.text: This guide walks you through creating your first project in MAGDA.

docs.title: Creating a New Project
docs.text:
1. Launch MAGDA
  2. Select File > New Project
  3. Choose a location to save your project

docs.title: Adding a Track
docs.text: Press Ctrl+T (or Cmd+T on macOS) to add a new track, or use Track > Add Track from the menu.

docs.title: Recording Audio
docs.text:
1. Configure your audio input in Settings > Audio
  2. Arm a track for recording by clicking its R button
  3. Press the Record button in the transport bar (or press R)
  4. Press Space to stop

docs.title: Navigating Between Views
docs.text:
Use the mode buttons in the footer bar at the bottom of the window to switch between views:

  • Live — Session view with clip launcher
  • Arrange — Timeline arrangement view
  • Mix — Full mixer with channel strips

docs.title: Saving Your Work
docs.text: Use Ctrl+S (or Cmd+S on macOS) to save. Use Ctrl+Shift+S (or Cmd+Shift+S) to save as a new file.

docs.title: System Requirements
docs.text: Minimum Recommended OS macOS 11+, Windows 10, Ubuntu 22.04 Latest stable release RAM 4 GB 8 GB+ Disk 200 MB 500 MB+ (for projects and audio files) Audio Built-in audio output Dedicated audio interface

docs.title: Download
docs.text: Download the latest release from the GitHub Releases page.

docs.title: Building from Source
docs.text: MAGDA is built with CMake and requires a C++20 compiler. See the README for full build instructions.

docs.title: Audio Setup
docs.text:
On first launch, go to Settings > Audio to configure:

  • Audio device — Select your audio interface or built-in output
  • Sample rate — 44100 Hz or 48000 Hz are common choices
  • Buffer size — Lower values reduce latency but increase CPU load

docs.title: Audio Settings
docs.text: Open audio settings from Settings > Audio Settings.

docs.title: Audio Device
docs.text:
• Output device — Select your audio interface or built-in output
  • Input device — Select the audio input for recording
  • Sample rate — 44100 Hz and 48000 Hz are common choices
  • Buffer size — Lower values reduce latency but increase CPU load

Tip

MAGDA automatically optimizes buffer sizes when switching views: Live mode uses the lowest latency, while Mix and Arrange modes use larger buffers for stability.

docs.title: Channel Configuration
docs.text:
• Active output channels — Choose which output channels to use (stereo pair or multi-channel)
  • Active input channels — Choose which input channels are available for recording

docs.title: MIDI
docs.text:
• MIDI input devices — Enable or disable connected MIDI controllers and keyboards
  • MIDI output devices — Enable or disable MIDI output to hardware synthesizers

docs.title: Preferred Device
docs.text: • Use as preferred device — Check this box to remember your device selection and restore it automatically on startup. If the preferred device is unavailable, MAGDA falls back to the system default.

docs.title: Menus
docs.text: MAGDA has eight top-level menus.

docs.title: File
docs.text: Item Description New Project Create a new empty project Open Project Open an existing project file Open Recent List of recently opened projects Close Project Close the current project Save Project Save the current project Save Project As Save the current project to a new file Export Audio Export/render the project to an audio file Quit Exit MAGDA (Windows/Linux only — use Cmd+Q on macOS)

docs.title: Edit
docs.text: Item Description Undo Undo the last action Redo Redo the last undone action Cut Cut selected clips or notes Copy Copy selected clips or notes Paste Paste from clipboard Duplicate Duplicate selected clips or tracks Delete Delete selected clips or notes Split / Trim Split clip at the playhead or trim to time selection Join Clips Join adjacent selected clips into one Render Selected Clip(s) Render selected clips to audio Render Time Selection Render the current time selection to audio Select All Select all clips or notes in the current context

docs.title: View
docs.text: Item Description Track Manager Open the Track Manager dialog (see below) Headers on the Right Toggle track headers to the right side of the arrangement Zoom In Increase horizontal zoom Zoom Out Decrease horizontal zoom Zoom to Fit Fit all content in the visible area Zoom Loop to Fit Zoom to show the loop region Zoom Selection to Fit Zoom to show the current selection Enter Full Screen Toggle full-screen mode

docs.title: Track Manager
docs.text:
The Track Manager lets you control which tracks are visible in each view. Open it from View > Track Manager.

Each track has a checkbox for each view mode: Live, Arrange, Mix, and Master. Toggle the checkboxes to show or hide individual tracks (including the master channel) per view. This is useful for keeping the mixer focused on relevant tracks or hiding utility tracks from the arrangement.

docs.title: Transport
docs.text: Item Description Play / Pause Toggle playback Stop Stop playback and return to last start position Record Start/stop recording Loop Toggle loop playback Go to Start Move playhead to the beginning Go to End Move playhead to the end

docs.title: Track
docs.text: Item Description Add Track Add a new track Add Group Track Add a new group track Add Aux Track Add a new aux/return track Delete Track Delete the selected track Duplicate Track Duplicate the selected track with content Duplicate Track Without Content Duplicate track structure without content Mute Track Toggle mute on the selected track Solo Track Toggle solo on the selected track

docs.title: Settings
docs.text: Item Description Preferences Open the preferences dialog Audio/MIDI Settings Open audio and MIDI device configuration Plugin Settings Open plugin settings (see Plugin Settings)

docs.title: Window
docs.text: Item Description Minimize Minimize the window Zoom Zoom the window to fill the screen Bring All to Front Bring all MAGDA windows to front

docs.title: Help
docs.text: Item Description Online Manual Open this manual in the browser About MAGDA Show version and credits

docs.title: Interface Overview
docs.text: MAGDA's window is divided into fixed regions that stay consistent across all three views (Live, Arrange, Mix).

docs.title: Transport Bar
docs.text: The transport bar sits at the top of the window and is always visible. It provides playback controls, tempo, time signature, loop/punch settings, and position displays. See Transport for details.

docs.title: Left Panel
docs.text:
The left panel contains:

  • Inspector — Context-sensitive properties for the selected track, clip, note, or device. See Inspector.
  • AI Assistant — Chat-based assistant for natural-language control. See AI Assistant.

docs.title: Central View
docs.text:
The central area displays one of three views depending on the active mode:

  • Session View — Grid-based clip launcher for real-time performance. See Session View.
  • Arrangement View — Traditional timeline for composing and editing. See Arrangement View.
  • Mixer View — Channel-strip mixer for balancing and routing. See Mixer View.

Switch between views using the mode buttons in the footer.

docs.title: Right Panel
docs.text:
The right panel contains two tools:

  • Plugin Browser — Browse, search, and drag plugins onto tracks
  • Media Explorer — Browse the file system, preview audio files, and drag them into the project

See Plugin & Media Browser for details.

docs.title: Bottom Panel
docs.text:
The bottom panel auto-switches based on the current selection:

  • Empty — Nothing selected
  • Piano Roll — When a MIDI clip is selected
  • Drum Grid — When a drum clip or DrumGrid device is selected
  • Waveform Editor — When an audio clip is selected
  • Track Chain — When a track or device is selected, showing the FX chain

See Editors for details.

docs.title: Footer
docs.text:
The footer bar at the bottom of the window contains:

  • Live / Arrange / Mix mode buttons — Switch between the three main views
  • Panel visibility toggles

docs.title: Panel Behavior
docs.text:
All side and bottom panels are:

  • Collapsible — Click the panel header or use keyboard shortcuts to show/hide
  • Resizable — Drag the panel edges to adjust width or height

docs.title: Plugin Settings
docs.text: The Plugin Settings dialog manages VST3 plugin directories, scanning, and exclusions. Open it from Settings > Plugin Scan.

docs.title: System Plugin Directories
docs.text:
Lists the default system paths where MAGDA looks for VST3 plugins. These are read-only and determined by the operating system:

  • /Users/<name>/Library/Audio/Plug-Ins/VST3 (user)
  • /Library/Audio/Plug-Ins/VST3 (system)

docs.title: Custom Plugin Directories
docs.text: Add extra folders for MAGDA to search when scanning for plugins. Use the Add... button to browse for a directory and Remove to delete the selected entry.

docs.title: Scanning
docs.text:
• Scan for Plugins — Start a full scan of all listed directories. A progress bar shows scan progress.
  • View Scan Report — Open the log from the most recent scan, showing which plugins were found, loaded, or rejected.

docs.title: Excluded Plugins
docs.text: Plugins that failed to load or were manually excluded appear here with the reason and date. Use Remove Selected to re-enable individual plugins or Reset All to clear the entire exclusion list.

docs.title: Preferences
docs.text: Open the Preferences dialog from Settings > Preferences. The dialog has five tabs.

docs.title: General
docs.text:
• Default zoom level — Initial horizontal zoom when opening a project
  • Timeline units — Choose between bars/beats and time (minutes:seconds)

docs.title: UI
docs.text:
• Panel visibility defaults — Choose which panels are shown on startup
  • Behavior settings — Configure UI interaction preferences

docs.title: Rendering
docs.text:
• Default format — Choose the default audio format for renders (WAV, AIFF, FLAC, OGG)
  • Sample rate — Default sample rate for rendered files
  • Bit depth — Default bit depth for rendered files

docs.title: AI
docs.text:
• API key — Enter your API key to enable the AI Assistant

See AI Assistant for more on setting up and using the AI chat.

docs.title: Shortcuts
docs.text:
• Keyboard shortcuts — View and customize all keyboard shortcuts
  • Reset to defaults — Restore the default shortcut mappings

See Keyboard Shortcuts for the full default shortcut list.

docs.title: Linking Parameters
docs.text: Linking connects modulation sources (modulators or macros) to target parameters on devices.

docs.title: Link Mode
docs.text:
Link mode is the workflow for creating modulation connections:

  1. Enter link mode — Click the link button in the toolbar or press the link mode shortcut
  2. Select a source — Click a modulator or macro knob
  3. Click a target — Click any device parameter knob or slider
  4. Adjust the link — Set the modulation amount and polarity
  5. Exit link mode — Press Esc or click the link button again

docs.title: Drag and Drop
docs.text: You can also create links by dragging from a modulation source directly onto a target parameter. This is a shortcut that skips the link-mode workflow.

docs.title: Link Settings
docs.text:
Each link has the following properties:

  • Amount — How much the source affects the target (0–100%)
  • Bipolar — When enabled, modulation swings both above and below the parameter's current value. When disabled, modulation only adds to the current value.

docs.title: Modulation Matrix
docs.text:
The modulation matrix provides an overview of all active links on a track:

  • Rows represent sources (modulators and macros)
  • Columns represent target parameters
  • Cells show the link amount — click to edit or remove

docs.title: Visual Indicators
docs.text:
When modulation is active:

  • Animated rings appear around modulated parameter knobs, showing the modulation range in real time
  • Color coding distinguishes between different modulation sources
  • Parameters with active modulation links display a small indicator dot

docs.title: Macros
docs.text: Macros are user-defined control knobs that provide quick, unified access to multiple device parameters.

docs.title: Layout
docs.text: Each track has 16 macro knobs organized across 2 pages (8 knobs per page). Macros are visible in the track's device chain and in the Inspector when the track is selected.

docs.title: Range
docs.text: All macro knobs output a normalized 0–1 range. The mapping to each target parameter's actual range is handled by the modulation link (see Linking Parameters).

docs.title: Naming
docs.text:
• Click a macro label to rename it (e.g., "Filter Cutoff", "Drive Amount")
  • Names are displayed on the knob and in the modulation matrix

docs.title: Assigning Parameters
docs.text:
To connect a macro to a parameter:

  1. Enter link mode (see Linking Parameters)
  2. Select the macro as the modulation source
  3. Click the target parameter
  4. Adjust the modulation amount and polarity

A single macro can control multiple parameters simultaneously — for example, one "Brightness" knob could increase filter cutoff, reduce reverb wet, and boost high-shelf EQ gain at the same time.

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Map the most important synth parameters to a few knobs for live performance
  • Create unified controls that coordinate multiple effects at once
  • Expose simple controls for complex multi-device setups

docs.title: Modulators
docs.text: Modulators are signal generators that modulate device parameters over time. Each device and rack can have its own set of modulators.

docs.title: LFO
docs.text: A low-frequency oscillator that cycles through a waveform shape.

docs.title: Waveforms
docs.text:
• Sine
  • Triangle
  • Sawtooth
  • Square
  • Random (Sample & Hold)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• Rate — Speed of the LFO cycle (Hz or synced to tempo)
  • Depth — Modulation amount
  • Phase offset — Starting point in the waveform cycle (0°–360°)
  • Tempo sync — Lock the rate to musical divisions (1/4, 1/8, 1/16, etc.)
  • One-shot — Play the waveform once instead of looping

docs.title: Bezier Curve Shape
docs.text: A freely editable modulation shape drawn with bezier curves. Use this to create complex, custom modulation patterns that go beyond standard waveforms.

docs.title: Editing
docs.text:
• Add a point — Double-click on the curve
  • Move a point — Drag it to a new position
  • Adjust curvature — Drag the bezier handles to shape the curve between points
  • Delete a point — Double-click an existing point

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
• Rate — Speed of the curve cycle (Hz or synced to tempo)
  • Depth — Modulation amount
  • Tempo sync — Lock the rate to musical divisions

docs.title: Modulation Overview
docs.text: MAGDA provides two complementary modulation systems: Modulators and Macros. Both allow you to dynamically control device parameters without manual automation.

docs.title: Modulators
docs.text:
Modulators are signal generators that continuously vary a target parameter over time. MAGDA includes:

  • LFO — Low-frequency oscillator with multiple waveforms (sine, triangle, sawtooth, square, random)
  • Bezier Curve Shape — Freely editable modulation shape drawn with bezier curves for complex custom patterns

See Modulators for details.

docs.title: Macros
docs.text:
Macros are user-defined knobs that can control multiple parameters at once. Each track has 16 macro knobs (across 2 pages) that provide quick access to the most important parameters.

See Macros for details.

docs.title: Hierarchical Scope
docs.text: Modulators and macros are scoped to their parent track. A modulator on Track 1 can target any parameter within Track 1's device chain, but not parameters on other tracks.

docs.title: Multi-Target and Multi-Source
docs.text:
• A single modulator can drive multiple target parameters simultaneously
  • A single parameter can be driven by multiple modulation sources, with their effects combined

docs.title: Linking
docs.text: Parameters are connected to modulation sources using MAGDA's link mode. See Linking Parameters for the workflow.

docs.title: AI Assistant
docs.text: MAGDA includes a built-in AI chat assistant that lets you control the DAW using natural language.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The AI Assistant panel is located in the left panel. Type a request in natural language and the assistant translates it into actions:

  • "Add a MIDI track with a bass clip"
  • "Transpose the selected notes up an octave"
  • "Set the tempo to 120 BPM"
  • "Mute tracks 3 and 4"

The assistant is context-aware — it knows which tracks, clips, and devices exist in your project and what is currently selected.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. You type a natural-language request in the chat
  2. The assistant translates your request into MAGDA's internal DSL (domain-specific language)
  3. The DSL commands are executed as actions in the project
  4. The assistant confirms what was done

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
To enable the AI Assistant:

  1. Go to Settings > Preferences > AI
  2. Enter your API key
  3. Select a model

docs.title: Usage Tips
docs.text:
• Be specific: "Add a reverb to Track 2" works better than "make it sound spacey"
  • The assistant can handle multi-step requests: "Create 4 MIDI tracks and name them Kick, Snare, HiHat, Bass"
  • Use it for repetitive tasks: "Set all tracks to -6 dB"

docs.title: MAGDA DSL Reference
docs.text: The AI assistant translates natural language into MAGDA's domain-specific language (DSL). While you don't need to write DSL directly, understanding it can help you phrase better requests.

docs.title: Tracks
docs.text:
track(name="Bass", type="midi")           // Create a new track
track(id=1)                               // Reference existing track by index
track(name="Bass").track.set(volume_db=-3, pan=0.5, mute=true, solo=true)
track(name="Bass").select()               // Select track in UI
track(name="Bass").delete()               // Delete track

docs.title: Clips
docs.text:
track(name="Bass").clip.new(bar=1, length_bars=4)     // Create MIDI clip
track(name="Bass").clip.rename(index=0, name="Intro") // Rename clip
track(name="Bass").clip.delete(index=0)                // Delete clip

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
.notes.add(pitch=C4, beat=0, length=1, velocity=100)
.notes.delete()
.notes.transpose(semitones=5)
.notes.set_pitch(pitch=F1)
.notes.set_velocity(value=80)
.notes.quantize(grid=0.25)       // 0.25=16th, 0.5=8th, 1.0=quarter
.notes.resize(length=0.5)

docs.title: Chords
docs.text:
.notes.add_chord(root=C4, quality=major, beat=0, length=1, velocity=100, inversion=0)

Supported qualities: major, minor, dim, aug, sus2, sus4, dom7, maj7, min7, dim7, dom9, maj9, min9, dom11, min11, dom13, min13, add9, add11, add13, 6, min6, 7b5, 7sharp5, 7b9, 7sharp9, min7b5, power

docs.title: Arpeggios
docs.text:
.notes.add_arpeggio(root=C4, quality=minor, beat=0, step=0.5,
                    note_length=0.5, velocity=100, pattern=up, fill=true)

Patterns: up, down, updown. Set fill=true to repeat the pattern to fill the entire clip, or beats=N to fill a specific number of beats.

docs.title: Effects
docs.text:
track(name="Vocals").fx.add(name="reverb")
track(name="Vocals").fx.add(name="Pro-Q 3", format="VST3")

Built-in effects: eq, compressor, reverb, delay, chorus, phaser, filter, utility, pitch shift, ir reverb

docs.title: Selection and Filtering
docs.text:
// Select notes by condition
.notes.select(note.pitch == C4)
.notes.select(note.velocity > 100)

// Select clips by condition
.clips.select(clip.length_bars >= 2)

// Bulk operations on matching tracks
filter(tracks, track.name == "Drums").track.set(mute=true)
filter(tracks, track.name == "Drums").for_each(.fx.add(name="compressor"))

docs.title: Example: Full Workflow
docs.text:
// Create a synth track with a 4-bar chord progression
track(name="Synth", type="midi")
  .clip.new(bar=1, length_bars=4)
  .notes.add_chord(root=C4, quality=major, beat=0, length=4)
  .notes.add_chord(root=F4, quality=major, beat=4, length=4)
  .notes.add_chord(root=G4, quality=major, beat=8, length=4)
  .notes.add_chord(root=Am3, quality=minor, beat=12, length=4)

// Add an arpeggiated lead
track(name="Lead", type="midi")
  .clip.new(bar=1, length_bars=4)
  .notes.add_arpeggio(root=C4, quality=major, step=0.25, pattern=updown, fill=true)

// Add reverb and set volume
track(name="Lead").fx.add(name="reverb").track.set(volume_db=-6)

docs.title: Plugin & Media Browser
docs.text: The left panel contains two browser tools for finding and adding content to your project.

docs.title: Plugin Browser
docs.text: The Plugin Browser shows all available audio plugins organized in a tree view.

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text:
• Tree view — Plugins organized by manufacturer and category
  • Search — Type to filter by plugin name
  • Favorites — Star plugins for quick access

docs.title: Adding Plugins
docs.text:
• Drag and drop — Drag a plugin from the browser onto a track header or into the FX chain to add it
  • Double-click — Add the plugin to the currently selected track's FX chain

docs.title: Supported Formats
docs.text:
• VST3
  • Audio Units (AU) — macOS only
  • VST (legacy)

docs.title: Rescanning
docs.text: If a newly installed plugin doesn't appear, use Settings > Plugin Scan to re-scan your plugin directories.

docs.title: Media Explorer
docs.text: The Media Explorer lets you browse files on your system and preview audio before importing.

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text:
• File browser — Navigate your file system with a tree view
  • Path bar — Click to jump to a specific directory

docs.title: Preview
docs.text:
• Click an audio file to preview it through your monitor output
  • Preview plays in sync with the transport tempo when applicable

docs.title: Filters
docs.text:
• Filter by file type (audio, MIDI, project)
  • Sort by name, date, or size

docs.title: Importing
docs.text:
• Drag and drop — Drag files from the explorer onto a track or clip slot to import
  • Supported audio formats: WAV, AIFF, FLAC, OGG, MP3
  • Supported MIDI formats: .mid files

docs.title: Editors
docs.text: The bottom panel displays an editor that auto-switches based on the current selection.

docs.title: Piano Roll
docs.text:
Displayed when a MIDI clip is selected. Provides a grid for editing notes:

  • Horizontal axis — Time (bars and beats)
  • Vertical axis — Pitch (MIDI note numbers, with piano keyboard on the left)
  • Click to add a note
  • Drag a note to move it in time or pitch
  • Drag edges to resize note length
  • Velocity lane at the bottom for editing note velocities

Header controls

  • Grid resolution — Draggable numerator/denominator for grid subdivision
  • AUTO — Automatically adjust grid resolution based on zoom level
  • SNAP — Toggle snap-to-grid

Footer controls

  • Velocity — Toggle the velocity/MIDI lane at the bottom of the editor

docs.title: Drum Grid Editor
docs.text:
Displayed when a drum clip is selected or a DrumGrid device is active. Shows a step-sequencer-style grid:

  • Rows represent drum pads / MIDI notes
  • Columns represent time steps
  • Click cells to toggle hits on/off
  • Velocity per hit is adjustable

Header controls

  • Grid resolution — Draggable numerator/denominator for grid subdivision
  • AUTO — Automatically adjust grid resolution based on zoom level
  • SNAP — Toggle snap-to-grid

Footer controls

  • Velocity — Toggle the velocity lane at the bottom of the editor

See Drum Grid for details on the DrumGrid device.

docs.title: Waveform Editor
docs.text:
Displayed when an audio clip is selected. Shows the audio waveform with:

  • Zoom and scroll within the clip
  • Fade handles at clip edges for fade-in/fade-out
  • Warp markers for time-stretching (when warp is enabled)
  • Selection for cutting, copying, or rendering portions
  • Clip properties panel (right side) — Inline access to audio properties without switching to the Inspector

Header controls

  • ABS / REL — Toggle between absolute (timeline) and relative (clip) time mode
  • Grid resolution — Draggable numerator/denominator for grid subdivision
  • SNAP — Toggle snap-to-grid
  • GRID — Toggle grid line visibility

docs.title: Track Chain
docs.text:
Displayed when a track or device is selected. Shows the track's FX chain as a horizontal strip of devices:

  • Drag devices to reorder them in the chain
  • Click a device to select it and show its parameters in the Inspector
  • Drag plugins from the Plugin Browser onto the chain to add them
  • Right-click a device for options: bypass, remove, replace, move to rack
  • Racks are shown as expandable containers with nested parallel chains

Header controls

  • Rack — Create a new rack on the track
  • Tree — Open the track tree, a hierarchical view of all devices and racks
  • Track name, M (mute), S (solo), volume, pan — Quick access to track controls
  • Bypass — Bypass the entire track chain

docs.title: Inspector
docs.text: The Inspector panel is on the right side of the window. It displays context-sensitive properties for the currently selected item.

docs.title: Track Inspector
docs.text:
Displayed when a track is selected. Shows:

  • Track name — Click to rename
  • Track color — Click the color swatch to choose a custom color, or leave as auto-assigned
  • Volume and pan — Numeric readouts and controls
  • Input routing — Select audio/MIDI input source
  • Output routing — Select audio/MIDI output destination
  • Sends — List of send slots with level controls

docs.title: Clip Inspector
docs.text:
Displayed when a clip is selected. Shows:

  • Clip name — Click to rename
  • Clip color — Click the color swatch to choose a custom color
  • Position — Start time, end time, and phase offset on the timeline
  • Loop settings — Loop on/off, loop start, loop length

docs.title: Audio Properties
docs.text:
For audio clips, the inspector shows additional controls:

  • Warp / Beat — Toggle time-stretch mode; select algorithm (e.g. SoundTouch HQ)
  • Tempo — Source BPM and beat length detection
  • Transient Detection — Sensitivity slider for transient markers
  • Pitch — Transpose in semitones
  • Mix — Volume, pan, and gain controls
  • Reverse — Reverse the clip audio
  • Launch Quantize — Quantize setting for Session View clip launching

docs.title: Note Inspector
docs.text:
Displayed when one or more MIDI notes are selected in the Piano Roll. Shows:

  • Pitch — MIDI note number and name
  • Velocity — Note velocity (0–127)
  • Start — Note start position
  • Length — Note duration

When multiple notes are selected, the inspector shows ranges (e.g., "C3–G5") and allows batch editing.

docs.title: Device Inspector
docs.text:
Displayed when a device (plugin or built-in) is selected in the track chain. Shows:

  • Device name — Click to rename the instance
  • Device type — Plugin format and category
  • Parameters — All exposed parameters with knobs/sliders

docs.title: Project Files
docs.text:
MAGDA saves projects in Tracktion Engine's Edit format (.tracktionedit), an XML-based format that stores:

  • Track layout and settings
  • Clip references and positions
  • Plugin chains and parameters
  • Automation data

docs.title: Audio Formats
docs.text:
MAGDA supports the following audio formats for import and export:

Format Import Export WAV Yes Yes AIFF Yes Yes FLAC Yes Yes OGG Yes Yes MP3 Yes No

docs.title: MIDI
docs.text: Standard MIDI files (.mid) can be imported and exported.

docs.title: Transport
docs.text: Shortcut Action Space Play / Stop R Record Home Return to start

docs.title: Editing
docs.text: Shortcut Action Ctrl+Z / Cmd+Z Undo Ctrl+Shift+Z / Cmd+Shift+Z Redo Ctrl+X / Cmd+X Cut Ctrl+C / Cmd+C Copy Ctrl+V / Cmd+V Paste Ctrl+D / Cmd+D Duplicate clips or track Ctrl+Shift+D / Cmd+Shift+D Duplicate track (without content) Ctrl+A / Cmd+A Select all Del / Backspace Delete selected clips or track Ctrl+E / Cmd+E Split / trim at cursor or selection Ctrl+J / Cmd+J Join clips Ctrl+B / Cmd+B Render clip to audio Ctrl+Shift+B / Cmd+Shift+B Render time selection

docs.title: Tracks
docs.text: Shortcut Action Ctrl+T / Cmd+T Add new track M Toggle mute on selected track Shift+S Toggle solo on selected track

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: Shortcut Action Ctrl++ / Cmd++ Zoom in Ctrl+- / Cmd+- Zoom out Ctrl+Shift+L / Cmd+Shift+L Set loop from clip bounds

docs.title: File
docs.text: Shortcut Action Ctrl+S / Cmd+S Save Ctrl+Shift+S / Cmd+Shift+S Save as

docs.title: Other
docs.text: Shortcut Action Esc Exit link mode / clear edit cursor

docs.title: No Audio Output
docs.text:
1. Check Settings > Audio and verify the correct output device is selected
  2. Make sure your system volume is turned up
  3. Check that no tracks are muted and the master fader is up
  4. Try a different sample rate (e.g., 44100 Hz or 48000 Hz)

docs.title: High Latency
docs.text:
1. Lower the buffer size in Settings > Audio
  2. Use an ASIO driver (Windows) or Core Audio (macOS) for best performance
  3. Close other audio applications
  4. Switch to Live mode for the lowest latency profile

docs.title: Crashes on Startup
docs.text:
1. Try deleting the configuration file (magda_config.txt) to reset settings
  2. Check the GitHub Issues for known problems
  3. File a bug report with your system details and crash log

docs.title: Plugin Issues
docs.text:
1. Verify the plugin is compatible with your OS and architecture
  2. Re-scan plugins from Settings > Plugins
  3. Try loading the plugin in a fresh project

Tip

If your issue isn't listed here, check the GitHub Issues page or open a new issue.
